title: Mirror Lamps
desc: By evening the lounge needs to look effortless, which means someone has to make the light behave first.

steps.text:
The Mipedim Lounge is most honest in the hour before it fills. Without voices and veils and careful laughter to distract from it, the room reveals its construction: low couches arranged like invitations, polished brass mirrors hung to multiply lampglow without ever making the place feel fully exposed, bowls of pale stone set out to catch cool water and reflected light in equal measure. When evening service begins, all of that has to look natural. Before it begins, it is work.

The lamp keeper hands you a cloth soft as sifted silk and a tray of trimmed wicks. "Polish the hanging mirrors if you want the room to widen," she says. "Reset the lamp line if you want the light to flatter everyone equally, which is impossible but still expected. Either task has to be done before the first noble arrives pretending not to care."

options.label: Polish the hanging mirrors and brighten the room
Reset the lamp line and trim the evening wicks

steps.text:
The mirrors are not meant to be obvious. Their job is to carry glow into alcoves, catch the edge of a polished scale, and make the whole lounge feel deeper than it is without ever looking like a hall of tricks. Dust and skin oil ruin that subtlety first. You work from chain to chain, cleaning each plate until the reflected light sharpens and the red cushions below begin taking on warmer color.

By the final mirror, the room has changed completely. What felt merely comfortable now feels deliberate, every corner holding just enough brightness to suggest attention without demanding it. The keeper steps back, studies the softened reflections, and seems satisfied. "Good," she says. "Now the lounge can pretend it always looked this way."

options.label: Hang the cloth over your arm and leave the mirrors clear

steps.text:
The lamp line runs in low tiers through the lounge, each bowl small enough to avoid heat glare and bright enough, when properly trimmed, to make scales shine and shadows behave. You pinch and reset the wicks one by one, balancing the oil so no flame smokes and no alcove burns brighter than its neighbor unless the room's social map requires it. Here, sometimes it does.

When the last wick takes, the whole lounge settles into evening. Light pools along the low tables, glides off the mirror chain, and catches the fine grain in the carved screens as if it had been waiting there all day. The keeper checks the main seating arc and gives a small approving nod. "There. Enough light for status, not enough for honesty." In the Lounge, that is a very respectable balance.

options.label: Cap the oil tray and leave the lamps burning evenly
